---
title: "Why Matching Speaking Notes"
subtitle: "Facilitator notes for the concept deck"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-links:
      - text: Why We Do Matching deck
        href: why_matching.html
      - text: Presenter script
        href: why_matching_script.html
      - text: DAG appendix
        href: ../articles/dag_bad_controls_appendix.html
      - text: Main report
        href: ../articles/matching_methods_report.html
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

## How To Use These Notes

Use this page as the prompt-style facilitator notes for the shorter concept deck.

- Keep it open beside the deck if you do not want presenter view.
- Use the presenter script if you want fuller wording rather than prompts.
- Use it as a `20-25` minute conceptual opener before the workshop deck or before a lab.
- Open the DAG appendix separately if you want a longer detour on good and bad controls.

## Recommended Setup

Open these pages before the session starts:

- [Why We Do Matching deck](why_matching.html)
- [Presenter script](why_matching_script.html)
- [DAG and Bad Controls Appendix](../articles/dag_bad_controls_appendix.html)
- [Matching Methods Report](../articles/matching_methods_report.html)

## 20-Minute Run Of Show

| Time | Slides | Aim |
|---|---|---|
| `0-3` | Title, Session Promise, 20-Minute Arc | Set the claim and the structure |
| `3-7` | Fundamental problem, comparison group, `RCT` benchmark | Explain the counterfactual problem and the best-case comparison |
| `7-12` | Raw comparisons, confounder slides, why match | Show why observational differences are biased |
| `12-17` | Workflow, subclassification, controls, weighting bridge | Explain what matching is trying to do |
| `17-20` | Assumptions, limits, practical standard | End with disciplined use rather than salesmanship |

## Slide-By-Slide Notes

### Title Slide

- `Purpose:` Open with the claim that matching is a response to a causal-design problem, not a software feature.
- `Say:` This talk is not about how to run one algorithm. It is about why matching exists at all.
- `Transition:` Move straight into the session promise so the room knows the deck is conceptual and finite.

### Session Promise

- `Purpose:` Tell the audience what the deck will and will not do.
- `Say:` We are going to start from the missing counterfactual, move through confounding, and only then discuss matching.
- `Emphasis:` Keep saying that matching is a design tool, not a magic causal stamp.
- `Transition:` Use that framing to justify the short run-of-show slide.

### 20-Minute Arc

- `Purpose:` Give the audience a map and keep yourself moving.
- `Say:` The first segment is the causal problem and the experimental benchmark, the middle segment is confounding plus matching logic, and the last segment is responsible use.
- `Transition:` Move to the fundamental problem immediately after the timing slide.

### Start With The Fundamental Problem

- `Purpose:` Ground the deck in potential outcomes rather than technique.
- `Say:` For each unit, one outcome is observed and one is missing. That missing outcome is the counterfactual.
- `Say:` Matching matters because it tries to build a credible stand-in for that missing outcome.
- `Transition:` Move to the comparison-group slide by saying that every causal design is really solving this problem one way or another.

### Why We Need A Comparison Group

- `Purpose:` Translate the abstract potential-outcomes idea into a practical design task.
- `Say:` The analyst is always trying to find or build the best available comparison for what did not happen.
- `Prompt:` Ask what makes a comparison group credible. You want answers like similarity, pre-treatment comparability, and common support.
- `Transition:` Move to the `RCT` benchmark slide by saying that the cleanest comparison is one we designed rather than inherited.

### Best Benchmark: Randomization

- `Purpose:` Put the experimental benchmark up front rather than treating it as an afterthought.
- `Say:` If treatment is randomized, the groups are comparable in expectation. That is why experiments are the benchmark.
- `Say:` But do not oversell them: attrition, noncompliance, and external-validity problems still remain.
- `Transition:` Move to the next slide by saying that matching only enters because most policy questions do not begin from this best-case design.

### Why Matching Enters The Story

- `Purpose:` Explain why the deck is about matching rather than experiments.
- `Say:` In many real evaluations, treatment has already happened and the comparison group is not automatically credible.
- `Say:` Matching is a second-best design repair for those settings, not a substitute for a better design when one exists.
- `Transition:` Then explain why the raw observational comparison is biased.

### Why Raw Comparisons Mislead

- `Purpose:` Explain the failure mode in observational work.
- `Say:` Treatment assignment is often related to need, motivation, eligibility, geography, or timing, and those same features may affect the outcome.
- `Say:` So the raw difference in outcomes bundles treatment effects together with baseline differences.
- `Transition:` Move to the confounder slide by naming that bundle explicitly as confounding.

### The Confounder Problem

- `Purpose:` Define confounding cleanly before showing a graph.
- `Say:` A confounder is a common cause of treatment and outcome. This is why association and causation separate.
- `Transition:` Move to the backdoor-path slide so the room can see the confounding structure directly.

### Backdoor Path

- `Purpose:` Make the core confounding structure visible.
- `Say:` This is the problem matching is trying to repair: a common cause points into both treatment and outcome.
- `Say:` Read the path aloud as `D <- X -> Y`.
- `Transition:` Move to the next slide by saying that once the bad path is visible, the design task becomes mechanical.

### What This Means For Design

- `Purpose:` Translate the backdoor idea into a design requirement without relying on a visual DAG.
- `Say:` If treated and untreated units differ on `Prior need`, the raw gap is confounded, but do not overstate the sign of the bias.
- `Say:` The direction depends on whether `Prior need` pushes the outcome up or down.
- `Say:` A credible design has to block the backdoor path by conditioning on the right pre-treatment variables.
- `Say:` This is the link between causal reasoning and matching.
- `Transition:` Move to the blocked-backdoor slide before defining matching itself.

### Block The Backdoor

- `Purpose:` Show the shared target behind multiple adjustment methods.
- `Say:` Once we condition on the confounder, the remaining open path is the causal one from treatment to outcome.
- `Say:` Regression, matching, stratification, and weighting are all trying to achieve this same design condition.
- `Transition:` Move to the matching-definition slide with the phrase "compare like with like, but only on the variables that matter."

### Matching In One Sentence

- `Purpose:` Give the shortest defensible definition of matching.
- `Say:` Matching compares treated units to untreated units that are similar on relevant observed pre-treatment covariates.
- `Warning:` Stress that "observed" is doing a lot of work in that sentence.
- `Transition:` Then explain why this approach remains attractive in applied work.

### Why Match?

- `Purpose:` Give the sharpest practical reason for matching.
- `Say:` The problem is not only imbalance. The problem is that imbalance makes the estimate depend too heavily on model choice.
- `Say:` Use the chain out loud: imbalance to model dependence to researcher discretion to bias.
- `Say:` Matching helps by removing or down-weighting the least credible comparisons before the model summarizes the data.
- `Transition:` Move to the next slide by saying that this is why design transparency matters operationally, not just philosophically.

### Why Matching Appeals In Practice

- `Purpose:` Show the operational value without overselling it.
- `Say:` Matching makes the comparison group more visible, reduces hidden extrapolation, and makes design diagnostics unavoidable.
- `Prompt:` Ask whether people trust a coefficient table more or less when they cannot see the comparison group.
- `Transition:` Move to the two-step workflow so the room sees how matching fits into an analysis rather than replacing it.

### Design In Two Steps

- `Purpose:` Separate preprocessing from estimation.
- `Say:` First redesign the comparison. Then estimate on that redesigned sample or weighting scheme.
- `Emphasis:` This is the easiest way to stop matching from dissolving into endless specification searching.
- `Transition:` Move to subclassification as the conceptual starting point for the preprocessing stage.

### From Subclassification To Matching

- `Purpose:` Connect the method back to a simple stratification idea.
- `Say:` Subclassification is the cleanest version of the logic: compare treated and untreated units within the same cells.
- `Say:` Exact matching and approximate matching are extensions of that basic idea.
- `Transition:` Then explain why this simple logic becomes difficult in realistic data.

### The Curse Of Dimensionality

- `Purpose:` Explain why exact comparisons become infeasible.
- `Say:` Once many covariates matter, the number of cells explodes and support thins out.
- `Say:` Matching methods exist because the causal goal is simple but the data are not.
- `Transition:` Move to covariate selection by saying that once you cannot match on everything, choosing the right variables becomes central.

### Good Controls, Not All Controls

- `Purpose:` Stop the audience from adopting kitchen-sink adjustment logic.
- `Say:` The relevant question is not "what baseline variables do we have?" but "which variables help block confounding?"
- `Say:` This is why causal reasoning has to precede software.
- `Say:` Use a pre-treatment common cause, not a collider, and not a variable changed by treatment.
- `Transition:` Move to the collider slide so the room sees the main bad-control failure mode before returning to the broader payoff.

### Collider Warning

- `Purpose:` Show the most important bad-control trap.
- `Say:` A collider is not a confounder. Conditioning on it can create a spurious association that was not there before.
- `Say:` This is why the selection of covariates must come from a causal story, not from a kitchen sink.
- `Transition:` Move into what matching is buying operationally.

### What Matching Is Buying

- `Purpose:` Summarize the gains from matching as design gains rather than identification gains.
- `Say:` Matching can reduce observed imbalance, make overlap problems visible, and force the analyst to report design costs plainly.
- `Warning:` This is a good place to say that transparency and validity are related but not identical.
- `Transition:` Move to weighting as the closest sibling approach before shifting to assumptions.

### Matching And Weighting Are Siblings

- `Purpose:` Clarify the relationship between the two method families.
- `Say:` Matching changes who stays in the sample. Weighting changes how much each unit counts.
- `Say:` Both are trying to repair the same comparison problem, and many matching procedures can be understood as weighted averages.
- `Transition:` Move to the implementation-logic slide before turning to assumptions.

### One Logic, Several Implementations

- `Purpose:` Tie Cunningham, Huntington-Klein, and the Mixtape Sessions framing together.
- `Say:` Regression, matching, stratification, and weighting differ in implementation, but all are trying to compare treated and untreated units after blocking backdoor paths with observables.
- `Say:` This is the shortest bridge from the causal story to the methods menu.
- `Transition:` Then move to the assumptions slide by saying that no implementation escapes the core identification constraints.

### Matching Is Still About Assumptions

- `Purpose:` Re-anchor the deck in the core assumptions.
- `Say:` Matching still relies on no important unmeasured confounders, usable overlap, actual observed balance, and a stable estimand.
- `Prompt:` Ask which of these assumptions is least testable. The answer should be omitted confounding.
- `Transition:` Move directly to the limitations slide by saying that these assumptions are exactly why matching has hard limits.

### What Matching Cannot Do

- `Purpose:` Prevent methodological overclaiming.
- `Say:` Matching cannot recover information you never measured, and it cannot rescue units with no support.
- `Say:` It is also not a substitute for a stronger design when one exists.
- `Transition:` Move to the "when not to match" slide so the room hears the design ranking explicitly.

### When Not To Match

- `Purpose:` State the method-ranking rule directly.
- `Say:` Use matching when it is the strongest defensible design available, not when it is merely available in software.
- `Say:` If overlap is too weak or the estimand drifts badly after pruning, that is information, not an inconvenience.
- `Transition:` Move to the "why still use it" slide so the deck does not end on prohibition alone.

### Why We Still Do It

- `Purpose:` Give the practical rationale after the warnings.
- `Say:` We still use matching because many important questions are observational, and matching can make weak designs more transparent and less dependent on pure regression form.
- `Transition:` End with the practical standard so the room leaves with a procedure, not just a concept.

### Practical Standard

- `Purpose:` Turn the conceptual story into an analyst workflow.
- `Say:` Define the estimand, choose covariates from the assignment story, inspect raw imbalance and overlap, adjust the design, then estimate.
- `Say:` After that, check uncertainty and sensitivity rather than treating the adjusted estimate as self-validating.
- `Emphasis:` This is the slide to reuse when teaching or reviewing applied matching work.
- `Transition:` Move to the lab-scope slide so the room knows what the follow-on practical session will and will not cover.

### What The Lab Focuses On

- `Purpose:` Narrow the practical methods menu before the lab starts.
- `Say:` In the core matching lab, we focus on exact matching and coarsened exact matching in `MatchIt`, then compare them with entropy balancing as a weighting benchmark.
- `Say:` We are not centering nearest-neighbor propensity-score matching or the broader set of advanced `MatchIt` variants on the first pass.
- `Say:` Point people to the [MatchIt documentation](https://kosukeimai.github.io/MatchIt/) if they want the broader method menu after the session.
- `Transition:` End on the closing line so the scope note feels like a practical handoff rather than a new conceptual branch.

### Closing Line

- `Purpose:` Leave one sentence in the room.
- `Say:` We do matching to reduce observed confounding by building a more credible comparison group, while staying honest about what the data still cannot prove.
- `Optional close:` Ask the room what assumption they would still worry about after a well-executed matching exercise.

## Minimal Version If Time Slips

If you lose `5-7` minutes, keep only this sequence:

1. `Start With The Fundamental Problem`
2. `Best Benchmark: Randomization`
3. `Why Raw Comparisons Mislead`
4. `Matching In One Sentence`
5. `Why Match?`
6. `Matching Is Still About Assumptions`
7. `Practical Standard`

## Final Facilitator Check

Before you leave the notes page, make sure the room can answer all three:

1. Why is the raw treated-control difference biased in observational data?
2. Which covariates belong in a matching design, and which do not?
3. What assumption still remains untestable after matching?
